name: boltdb/bolt
why: Single-file embedded KV with a B+tree on mmap, small enough to hold in your head — the cleanest MVCC-via-COW I've read in open source. Tag v1.3.1 is the last release before archive.

entry_points.label: Trace a Put through a write transaction
entry_points.question: Where does the copy-on-write actually happen, and how do in-flight readers see a consistent snapshot of the old tree while a writer is mutating pages?

entry_points.label: Understand the freelist
entry_points.question: How does Bolt reclaim freed pages without breaking readers that still hold pointers into the old MVCC snapshot?

name: golang/groupcache
why: Brad Fitzpatrick's distributed in-process cache. The singleflight trick — deduping concurrent fetches — is a small file with a big lesson.

entry_points.label: Read singleflight
entry_points.question: When N goroutines all ask for the same key at the same time, how does singleflight ensure only one actually fetches it, and what guarantees do the other N-1 callers get about the value they receive (and any error)?

entry_points.label: Read the consistent hash ring
entry_points.question: How does groupcache decide which peer owns a key, and what happens to the distribution when a peer is added or removed?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: BurntSushi/ripgrep
why: Andrew Gallant's grep replacement and a clinic in "fast for real users" — every interesting decision in tool building made out loud, from the regex engine choice to mmap heuristics to gitignore-aware filesystem walking.

entry_points.label: Architecture tour: a single grep through searcher and matcher
entry_points.question: What's the shape of a single grep run through ripgrep — main → walk → searcher → matcher → printer — and which boundary is where "this is fast" actually gets earned (literal-prefix detection, mmap vs streaming, Sink design)?

entry_points.label: Read the ignore crate
entry_points.question: How does ripgrep walk a directory tree while respecting .gitignore at every depth, and what's the relationship between the global, parent, and per-directory ignore patterns?

name: junegunn/fzf
why: The matching engine and scoring that make interactive fuzzy search feel instant on a million-line input. Surprisingly small.

entry_points.label: Read the matching algorithm
entry_points.question: What does FuzzyMatchV2 do differently from V1, and how do the scoring components (gap penalty, consecutive-match bonus, case-sensitivity bonus) compose into a single int?

name: kubernetes/kubernetes · pkg/scheduler
why: Kubernetes' decision-making for 'which pod goes on which node' — a real distributed scheduler with pluggable policies, living in `pkg/scheduler/` of the main kubernetes/kubernetes repo. Read it to see how predicate-and-priority became filter-and-score, and how a queue handles backoff, preemption, and unschedulable retries.

entry_points.label: Trace a pod from queue to bound
entry_points.question: When a new Pod hits the scheduler, what's the path from the scheduling queue through filter plugins, score plugins, and final binding — and where exactly does the algorithm decide a node is infeasible vs just lower-scoring?

name: etcd-io/raft
why: The standalone Raft implementation extracted from etcd. The whole protocol — leader election, log replication, snapshot, joint consensus — is a few thousand lines of Go you can hold in your head. The reference implementation many other systems consult.

entry_points.label: Read leader election
entry_points.question: When a node times out without hearing from the leader, what's the exact sequence of state transitions, RPCs sent and received, and tie-breaking until exactly one node becomes leader (or another timeout fires)?

entry_points.label: Read log replication and storage
entry_points.question: How does the leader commit a log entry — what does 'majority replicated' mean in this code, when does an entry become 'applied' vs just 'committed', and what's the contract with the storage interface?

name: fluent/fluent-bit
why: Log/metrics shipper at the heart of a million Kubernetes clusters. Event loop, pluggable input/filter/output chain, and a real budget for memory and CPU — read it to see what 'lightweight' actually buys in C.

entry_points.label: Trace a record through the pipeline
entry_points.question: From an input plugin emitting a record to an output plugin sending it elsewhere, what's the in-process path — when does the record become a chunk, where does routing happen, and what owns the memory at each hop?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: simonw/sqlite-utils
why: Pythonic wrapper over sqlite3 — read it to see what minimum API turns the stdlib module from awkward into pleasant.

entry_points.label: Read db.py top to bottom
entry_points.question: What's the minimum API surface that makes the Python sqlite3 module pleasant to use, and how does sqlite-utils handle the impedance between Python dicts and SQL columns (types, NULLs, primary keys, foreign keys)?

name: alicebob/miniredis
why: A test-time fake Redis written in Go — small enough to read in a sitting, big enough to teach you what the Redis wire protocol actually looks like.

entry_points.label: Trace a SET command from socket to reply
entry_points.question: How does miniredis read a RESP command off the socket, dispatch it, and write a RESP reply — and where exactly does the in-memory datastore get touched in that round-trip?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: ggerganov/whisper.cpp
why: A real transformer (the original Whisper) running end-to-end in plain C++ with no Python, no PyTorch, no ONNX runtime — just ggml tensors and a single source file. The clearest demonstration that "ML inference" is mostly arithmetic.

entry_points.label: Trace audio through to text
entry_points.question: From a 16kHz WAV input to a text transcript: where does the mel-spectrogram get computed, what tensors does the encoder produce, and how does the decoder loop choose tokens?

entry_points.label: Understand model loading and quantization
entry_points.question: When whisper-cpp loads a quantized model file (q5_0, q8_0, etc.), how is the weight data laid out in memory and how do the matrix-multiply kernels in ggml.c consume it without a full dequantization step?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: llvm/llvm-project (mem2reg)
why: LLVM's 'promote alloca to SSA register' pass — the optimization that turns memory-shaped IR into the SSA form every later pass operates on. The smallest LLVM transform that uses the dominator tree non-trivially.

entry_points.label: Read the promotion pass
entry_points.question: When mem2reg promotes a stack alloca to SSA registers, where does it insert the phi nodes and how does it use the dominator tree (via ForwardIDFCalculator) to know which load reads from which store?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: duckdb/duckdb (planner)
why: A modern analytical SQL planner small enough to finish reading. The Binder → LogicalPlan → Optimizer pipeline is well-named and easy to trace — read it to see how a textbook query plan actually looks in code.

entry_points.label: Trace a SELECT from parser to plan
entry_points.question: From `SELECT a, sum(b) FROM t WHERE c > 5 GROUP BY a`, what does Binder produce for the column references and the aggregate, and where in the LogicalPlan tree does the WHERE clause end up — above or below the aggregation?

name: rui314/chibicc
why: Rui Ueyama's small C compiler — compiles a real subset of C to x86-64 assembly, with each phase isolated in its own file. The cleanest hand-written compiler available to read.

entry_points.label: Trace a compilation top to bottom
entry_points.question: When chibicc compiles `int main() { return 1+2; }`, what does the token stream look like, what AST does parse.c build, and which x86-64 instructions does codegen.c emit for the addition and the return?

name: munificent/craftinginterpreters (tree-walker)
why: jlox is a complete language interpreter written across one book — the cleanest tree-walking interpreter I know, and the resolver pass teaches a lesson you don't get from textbooks.

entry_points.label: Trace an expression from source to value
entry_points.question: How does an expression like `(1 + 2) * 3` travel from source characters to a runtime double — what's the shape of the AST nodes (see Expr.java) in between, and where do precedence and associativity get encoded in Parser.java?

entry_points.label: Read the resolver
entry_points.question: Why does jlox need a separate variable-resolution pass — what bug does it fix, and how does the pass map a name to a static scope distance?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: mobile-shell/mosh
why: Mobile shell — replaces SSH for high-latency or roaming connections. The State Synchronization Protocol (SSP) it invented is the lesson: send "what the terminal should look like", not "what to type".

entry_points.label: Read the network layer
entry_points.question: How does mosh frame, sequence, and (re)transmit datagrams over a single UDP socket, and how does it survive a NAT rebinding or IP change without resetting the session?

entry_points.label: Read the state synchronization
entry_points.question: What is mosh actually sending across the wire — diffs of terminal state, or full snapshots, or both — and how does it decide?

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

entry_points.label: stub entry point
entry_points.question: stub

name: FiloSottile/age
why: A small, modern file-encryption tool whose entire format spec fits on one page. Read it to see how existing primitives (X25519, ChaCha20-Poly1305, HKDF) compose into a complete encrypted file format.

entry_points.label: Trace an X25519 encryption
entry_points.question: When `age -r <recipient> <file>` runs, how does the recipient's X25519 public key become a wrapped file key, and where in the code does the wrapped key get serialized into the file header?

name: busybox/busybox (coreutils/cat.c)
why: Every busybox applet is shaped the same way; cat.c is the smallest complete example of that shape.

entry_points.label: Read cat.c in full and identify the applet contract
entry_points.question: What does busybox demand of every applet (name, argv parsing, return code, output handling), and how does cat.c satisfy each of those in under 200 lines?

name: busybox/busybox (coreutils/wc.c)
why: Three counters in a single pass over arbitrary input — the shape of the loop is the lesson.

entry_points.label: Read wc.c and identify the multi-counter pattern
entry_points.question: Where in the main loop does wc commit to counting one of bytes / characters / words / lines, and how does the multibyte-input handling thread through that decision?

name: gnu/grep
why: The reference GNU grep. Read it to internalize how a real production regex tool composes DFA construction, Boyer-Moore literal scanning, and line buffering into a search loop that stays fast on adversarial inputs.

entry_points.label: Read the search loop
entry_points.question: How does GNU grep handle a regex pattern against a giant input stream — where does the DFA construction happen, and what tricks (Boyer-Moore, line buffering, byte-aware scans) does it use to stay fast on the common case?

name: Hobbit netcat (nc110)
why: Hobbit's original netcat, 1995 — a swiss-army-knife for TCP/UDP in under 1000 lines of K&R-flavored C. Predates 'nc' being a verb. The smallest complete network tool worth reading.

entry_points.label: Read main() and identify what netcat actually does
entry_points.question: How few lines of C does it take to open a TCP socket (connect or listen), copy stdin↔socket bidirectionally, and exit cleanly when either side closes? Then: what does netcat layer on top of that core to become useful?
